_comment: Two ways to run this, for two different questions.
`pnpm test:mutate` sets RMSL_SKIP_GPU, which turns off the layers that
hand shaders to real compilers and check what they compute. It finishes in
about twelve minutes. Stryker cannot set an environment variable from this
file, so running the binary directly skips nothing and pays the full cost
anyway.
`pnpm test:mutate:deep` leaves them on, and is the one to run when the
point is to find weak tests rather than to see a number. With those layers
off, a mutant can be reported as surviving that the whole suite would have
killed, so the list has to be checked by hand before it means anything —
which is the work the run was supposed to do. It needs the longer timeout:
at the default, a validated run is killed for being slow and counted as
killed, which raises the score and quietly drops the lead.
How much the deep run adds has not been measured reliably. Scoped
comparisons between the two did not reproduce, because a run is only
comparable to another over the same source and the same set of tests, and
ignoreStatic changes which mutants are reported at all when coverage
changes. Treat the deep run as the accurate one because it runs what is
actually shipped, not because of a number.
Both write to the same report, so a fast run after a deep one replaces a
trustworthy list with a provisional one.
